perturbation.original_text: 1.6 "Licensed Mark" means the stylized mark "H", as depicted in the USPTO Registration No. 5,283,875, attached hereto as Exhibit "A" and made a part hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the reference to USPTO Registration No. 5,283,875 and Exhibit "A", the definition of 'Licensed Mark' becomes incomplete. This omission creates an in-text contradiction because Article 2.1 states that the license includes the use and display of the Licensed Mark. Without a clear and complete definition (including the visual representation in Exhibit A), it becomes ambiguous what exactly the Licensee is permitted to use, potentially leading to disputes over what constitutes authorized use of the trademark. Furthermore, section 2.10 states that the user will cooperate with licensor to protect the trademark. However, if the trademark is not clearly defined as shown in Exhibit A. This becomes uncertain in enforcement
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 Licensor hereby grants Licensed Users a nonexclusive, nontransferable, nonsublicensable, royalty-free license, during the term of this Agreement, to use and display the Licensed Trade Name and the Licensed Mark in the United States solely in connection with the Licensee's corporate name and identifying mark.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the USPTO Registration No. 5,283,875 and Exhibit "A" from the definition of "Licensed Mark." This omission creates an in-text contradiction with section 2.1, which grants the licensee the right to "use and display the Licensed Trade Name and the Licensed Mark". Without the visual representation in Exhibit A, as originally stated in section 1.6, it becomes unclear what constitutes the "Licensed Mark", thereby creating ambiguity around the scope of the granted license in section 2.1. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' because essential language was removed from the original text, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 This Agreement and all rights and licenses granted under this Agreement shall terminate as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days, after: 3.2.1 Licensee is acquired by a third party; or 3.2.2 Licensor or any affiliate of Licensor ceases to manage Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 This Agreement and all rights and licenses granted under this Agreement shall terminate as soon as practicable after: 3.2.1 Licensee is acquired by a third party; or 3.2.2 Licensor or any affiliate of Licensor ceases to manage Licensee.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'but no longer than thirty (30) days' the termination clause becomes ambiguous. While 3.3 requires Licensee to cease using the Licensed Mark 'as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days, after termination', the modified 3.2 no longer specifies a deadline for the overall agreement termination. This creates a contradiction: Licensee must stop using the mark within 30 days of termination, but the agreement itself might theoretically continue indefinitely after the triggering event, or at least the deadline for termination is uncertain. This creates an in-text contradiction, because a termination timeline is not clearly defined, but a wind-down period is. This creates uncertainty in enforcement
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 Upon termination of this Agreement, Licensed Users shall immediately cease use of the Licensed Trade Name and Licensed Mark as soon as practicable, but no longer than thirty (30) days, after termination.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in 3.2 removes the 'but no longer than thirty (30) days' clause, creating ambiguity regarding the termination deadline of the entire agreement. This directly contradicts section 3.3, which mandates the licensee to cease using the licensed mark within thirty days 'after termination'. As the termination deadline in 3.2 is now undefined, the licensee is obligated to cease using the mark within 30 days of an event that triggers termination, but the termination itself might not occur within that 30-day period, rendering the enforcement of 3.3 unclear and creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Indemnification. Licensed Users, at Licensed Users' own expense, shall indemnify, hold harmless and defend Licensor, its affiliates, successors and assigns, and its and their directors, officers, employees and agents, against any claim, demand, cause of action, debt, expense or liability (including attorneys' fees and costs), to the extent that the foregoing (a) is based on a claim resulting solely from any service provided or offered by Licensed Users, (b) results from a material breach, or is based on a claim that, if true, would be a material breach, of this Agreement by Licensed Users, or (c) is based upon Licensed Users' unauthorized or improper use of the Licensed Mark.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Indemnification. Licensed Users shall indemnify Licensor, its affiliates, successors and assigns, and its and their directors, officers, employees and agents, against any claim, demand, cause of action, debt, expense or liability, to the extent that the foregoing (a) is based on a claim resulting solely from any service provided or offered by Licensed Users, (b) results from a material breach, or is based on a claim that, if true, would be a material breach, of this Agreement by Licensed Users, or (c) is based upon Licensed Users' unauthorized or improper use of the Licensed Mark.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'at Licensed Users' own expense', this creates an internal contradiction. If users are not responsible for their own expense, section 4.11 (Attorney's Fees) is now potentially contradicted. If the Licensor sues the licensee and is forced to pay the user's attorney fees, even if the Licensor wins, it creates an issue. The removal of the expense portion of 4.1 directly affects the reading of 4.11, since it would now fall on the licensor's side, rather than the user's expense, or at least creates ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.11 Attorney's Fees. In the event any suit or other legal proceeding is brought for the enforcement of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the Parties hereto agree that the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover from the other party upon final judgment on the merits reasonable attorneys' fees (and sales taxes thereon, if any), including attorneys' fees for any appeal, and costs incurred in bringing such suit or proceeding.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'at Licensed Users' own expense' in Section 4.1 directly contradicts the principle established in Section 4.11, which states that the prevailing party in a legal proceeding is entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees from the other party. By eliminating the explicit allocation of expenses to the Licensed Users in Section 4.1, the changed text introduces ambiguity regarding who is responsible for attorney fees in cases related to indemnification, conflicting with the clear provision in Section 4.11, potentially making enforcement unclear, and creating a legal inconsistency where the Licensor might have to cover the Licensed user fees even when prevailing in a legal battle related to indemnification.
